title: Disease burden from non-communicable diseases vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Disease burden to non-communicable diseases (NCDs), measured in DALYs (Disability-Adjusted Life Years) per 100,000 individuals versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption: Across countries in 2023, the disease burden from non-communicable diseases (NCDs) plotted against GDP per capita reveals a negative relationship where higher income levels coincide with lower disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) per 100,000 people. The vertical axis measures NCD burden ranging from 0 to 50,000 DALYs, while the horizontal axis displays GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale from roughly $1,000 to $100,000. Data points are color-coded by continent and sized by population, creating a visual distribution where low-income nations cluster in the upper-left quadrant and high-income nations occupy the lower-right. The highest disease burdens appear in countries with GDP per capita below $5,000, including Nauru at approximately 50,000 DALYs, the Solomon Islands and Marshall Islands near 40,000 DALYs, and Haiti, Myanmar, and Egypt between 30,000 and 35,000 DALYs. African nations such as the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Niger, Burundi, and Mozambique show values between 20,000 and 30,000 DALYs at income levels under $2,000. In the middle range, countries like India, Pakistan, and Indonesia sit between 20,000 and 25,000 DALYs with GDP per capita around $5,000 to $10,000. The United States stands as an outlier among high-income nations with a GDP per capita near $70,000 but a disease burden around 22,000 DALYs, higher than other wealthy countries. Conversely, Singapore, Japan, Germany, and Poland show the lowest burdens, all falling below 15,000 DALYs with GDP per capita exceeding $40,000. The overall pattern shows a downward slope where disease burden decreases as GDP per capita increases, with the steepest drop occurring between $2,000 and $20,000 income levels.
